summary: Marin team will prototype multiple LED configurations to resolve thermal, optical, and processing constraints within one week.

participants.name: Theo
participants.description: Strategic project manager for Marin. Coordinates all workstreams, tracks timeline, manages dependencies, and ensures the Day 30 gate is met.

participants.name: Palmer Luckey
participants.description: Optical engineer for Marin. Founded Oculus VR at 19, designed the Rift optics, founded Anduril Industries. Expert in display optics, Fresnel/TIR lenses, diffusion systems, and perceptual uniformity. Designs the optical stack: LED collimation, diffusion layers, reflective control, and seam blending.

participants.name: Jony Ive
participants.description: Legendary industrial designer. Responsible for enclosure design, thermal management, mechanical tolerances, manufacturing constraints, and the physical form factor of the Marin hemispherical display. Obsessed with how things feel in the hand, material selection, and the intersection of engineering and beauty.

participants.name: Nikola Tesla
participants.description: Electrical engineer for Marin. Designs power system (12-24V DC), STM32H7 control board, LED driver circuits (TLC5940/5971), Ethernet interface, thermal management, and PCB architecture.

participants.name: John Carmack
participants.description: Firmware and software engineer for Marin. Writes STM32H7 firmware (engine addressing, PWM, gamma, thermal), host API (REST/WebSocket), animation engine, OTA updates, and diagnostics.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Good morning team. Let's dive into Marin status. We're seeing strong development momentum with 117 commits on Hub in 48 hours, but I need clarity on our component research and optical stack progress. Palmer, where do we stand on LED selection and diffusion layer specs?

transcript.speaker: Palmer Luckey
transcript.message: Morning Theo. I've been deep in the LED collimation research, and I'm hitting some interesting tradeoffs. The high-CRI LEDs we need for proper color reproduction are running hotter than expected - about 15% more thermal load than the standard offerings. This is going to impact our power budget and potentially force us into active cooling territory.

transcript.speaker: Jony Ive
transcript.message: Palmer, that thermal concern aligns with what I've been modeling for the enclosure. If we're looking at active cooling, we need to fundamentally rethink the form factor. The elegance of passive thermal management through the aluminum hemisphere was a core design principle. Are we talking about fans, or could we explore vapor chambers?

transcript.speaker: Palmer Luckey
transcript.message: Vapor chambers might work, but they add complexity and cost. I'm wondering if we should reconsider the LED density approach instead. We could go with more LEDs at lower individual power - distribute the heat load across a larger surface area.

transcript.speaker: Nikola Tesla
transcript.message: More LEDs means more driver circuits, more complex PCB routing, and higher component costs. I've been designing around 144 addressable zones - going higher starts pushing the STM32H7 to its limits for real-time control. What's the minimum LED count that gives you acceptable optical uniformity, Palmer?

transcript.speaker: Palmer Luckey
transcript.message: That's the core question. I need to build some prototypes to test seam blending with different configurations. 144 zones might be workable if we nail the diffusion layer design, but I won't know until I can measure actual light distribution patterns.

transcript.speaker: John Carmack
transcript.message: From the firmware side, I can optimize for higher LED counts, but Tesla's right about the processing constraints. We're already running tight on the PWM refresh rates for smooth animation. If we bump the zone count significantly, we might need to consider frame rate compromises or more sophisticated temporal dithering.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: So we have a three-way constraint: thermal management, optical quality, and processing power. What's our path forward? Do we prototype multiple configurations or make a decision now based on theoretical models?

transcript.speaker: Jony Ive
transcript.message: I vote for prototypes. The thermal characteristics will vary dramatically based on the actual mechanical design - heat sink geometry, air flow patterns, material interfaces. We can't optimize in isolation. How quickly can we get test units built?

transcript.speaker: Palmer Luckey
transcript.message: If we go with off-the-shelf LED strips and basic 3D printed housings, I could have three configurations ready in a week. Won't be pretty, but it'll give us the thermal and optical data we need to make informed decisions.

transcript.speaker: Nikola Tesla
transcript.message: I can have driver boards ready to match Palmer's test configurations. Basic functionality only - no full feature set, but enough to characterize power consumption and thermal behavior under different loads.

transcript.speaker: John Carmack
transcript.message: And I'll write test firmware focused on stress testing - maximum brightness patterns, rapid color transitions, worst-case thermal scenarios. Better to find the breaking points early.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Excellent. That's our immediate sprint then. Palmer leads the optical prototypes, Tesla handles the electrical test boards, Carmack writes stress test firmware, and Jony models thermal scenarios for each configuration. Timeline is one week for initial data, then we converge on the final specification.

transcript.speaker: Jony Ive
transcript.message: One concern - if we end up needing active cooling, that fundamentally changes the industrial design. The seamless hemisphere becomes much more complex with ventilation requirements. Are we prepared for that possibility?

transcript.speaker: Palmer Luckey
transcript.message: Honestly, I'd prefer to solve this optically rather than mechanically. There might be clever ways to use the reflective control layer to reduce hot spots, or we could explore different LED wavelength combinations that are more thermally efficient.

transcript.speaker: Nikola Tesla
transcript.message: Or we could implement dynamic power scaling based on thermal feedback. Run hot zones at reduced power while maintaining overall illumination through smart zone balancing. The STM32H7 has multiple temperature sensors we could leverage.

transcript.speaker: John Carmack
transcript.message: That's definitely doable from a firmware perspective. I could implement thermal-aware power management that optimizes brightness distribution in real-time. Might actually result in better visual uniformity than static configurations.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Alright, we have our approach. Palmer, you're point on the prototype sprint. Everyone else supports with their domain expertise. Our goal is data-driven decisions on LED configuration, thermal management, and processing requirements by next Monday. Any blockers or dependencies I need to track?

transcript.speaker: Palmer Luckey
transcript.message: Just component availability. Some of the high-CRI LEDs have longer lead times. I'll start with what's readily available and note any performance differences.

transcript.speaker: Theo
transcript.message: Perfect. Let's execute this sprint and reconvene with hard data. The optical stack is our critical path to the Day 30 gate, so this is our top priority this week.

actionItems.text: Generate daily devotional content based on 8 faith dimensions
Draft faith lesson content for the Lever app (currently 7 lessons)
Draft feature spec for expanding Family tab (currently 455 lines)
Review family touchpoint tracker design for 6 family members

topPriorities: Complete LED configuration prototypes with thermal and optical testing
Validate STM32H7 processing capabilities under different LED densities
Determine if active cooling is required for final design

ceoDirectives.directive: What is Lever's direction? → Hybrid — personal tool now with product optionality built in
Keep faith content as separate standalone section (not merged into Family tab)
Keep cash liquid — no deployment until post-launch revenue data
App Store submission: full current build (all 4 tabs) as-is with faith lessons. Submit immediately after merge, ship modal as day-one update.
Paper trading MVP: manual entry (6 weeks), basic free / advanced analytics premium. Integrate into current Lever app finance tab.
Launch with delayed market data for both free and premium tiers. Real-time as future ultra-premium.
Org flattened to 8 C-suite agents. No permanent sub-agents — spawn dynamically via sessions_spawn.
